title: HTTP / HTTPS
objectives: Read a real HTTP request/response and understand every line
Know all the HTTP methods, when to use each, and which are 'safe' or 'idempotent'
Explain what HTTPS adds on top of HTTP
Answer HTTP questions confidently in interviews

sections.markdown:
**Imagine ordering food at a restaurant counter.**

1. You walk up and say: *'One masala dosa, please.'* — that's your **HTTP request**.
2. The cashier replies: *'OK, that'll be ready in 5 minutes'* — that's the **HTTP response**.
3. Sometimes they say *'Sorry, we're out'* (404) or *'Card declined'* (402) or *'System's down, come back later'* (503).
4. Each time you order, you start fresh — the cashier doesn't remember your name from last week. That's what **stateless** means.

**HTTPS is the same conversation, but you're speaking through a soundproof booth so nobody standing nearby can eavesdrop.**

**HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is a text-based request/response protocol at the application layer. The client asks; the server answers. **HTTPS** is HTTP carried inside a **TLS-encrypted channel** — the same conversation, but private and tamper-proof.

## Anatomy of a real HTTP request and response

HTTP is remarkably simple — it's just text over TCP. Here's an actual request/response pair, annotated:

sections.code:
GET /api/products?id=42 HTTP/1.1        <- Request line: METHOD PATH VERSION
Host: api.example.com                    <- Which host on this IP? (needed for shared hosting)
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...        <- Who are you? (token)
Accept: application/json                 <- What format do you want back?
User-Agent: MyApp/1.2.0                  <- Which client software?
                                          <- BLANK LINE separates headers from body
(No body for a GET request.)


HTTP/1.1 200 OK                          <- Status line: VERSION CODE MEANING
Content-Type: application/json           <- Response body format
Content-Length: 127                      <- How many bytes follow
Cache-Control: max-age=60                <- Cacheable for 60 seconds
Date: Wed, 01 Jul 2026 09:30:00 GMT      <- When it was sent
                                          <- BLANK LINE separates headers from body
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "GS1 Widget",
  "price": 199
}                                        <- The response body

sections.markdown: The whole thing is just plain text — you can literally type it into a `telnet` session by hand and get a real response. That's why HTTP became the language of the internet: it's readable, debuggable, and easy to implement.

## HTTP methods — 'verbs' for talking to the server

Each method tells the server what kind of action you want. Two adjectives matter for interviews: **safe** and **idempotent**.

**'Safe' vs 'idempotent' — the trap question:**

- **Safe** = does not change server state. Only GET, HEAD, OPTIONS are safe.
- **Idempotent** = calling it N times has the same effect as calling it once. GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS are idempotent. **POST and PATCH are not.**

**Why it matters:** if a POST request times out, the client can't safely retry — it might create two orders. But a PUT can always be retried safely. This is why real APIs (like Stripe) require an *idempotency key* header on POST requests — so retries are safe.

## Status codes — the ones you must memorize

Status codes come in ranges. The first digit tells you the category.

rows: 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 429 Too Many Requests
500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable, 504 Gateway Timeout

sections.markdown:
**401 vs 403 — the classic trap:**

- **401 Unauthorized** — *'I don't know who you are.'* The client didn't send credentials, or they're wrong. Log in and try again.
- **403 Forbidden** — *'I know who you are, but you don't have permission for this.'* Logging in again won't help; the user just isn't allowed.

Both are 4xx (client's problem), but the fix is different.

## HTTP versions — the evolution

The protocol has gone through three major versions, each solving the previous one's biggest problem.

In practice, your browser and most cloud CDNs now use HTTP/2 or HTTP/3. Your Flutter app's Dio/`http` package speaks whichever the server offers. You rarely code differently — but performance is very different, especially on lossy mobile networks.

## Stateless — the most misunderstood word in HTTP

HTTP is **stateless**: the server does not remember you between requests. Every request is a fresh conversation.

So how do you 'stay logged in'?

- **Cookies** — the server sends you an ID; you send it back on every request.
- **Tokens (JWT)** — you present a signed proof of who you are on every request.
- **Sessions** — a server-side store keyed by a cookie.

The magic is that from HTTP's point of view, every request still stands alone. State is layered on top.

## HTTPS in one paragraph

HTTPS = HTTP inside a **TLS-encrypted tunnel**. Before any HTTP happens, the client and server do a TLS handshake: exchange certificates, verify identity, agree on a symmetric key. From then on, everything (headers, body, URLs, cookies) is encrypted. An attacker on the same Wi-Fi sees only ciphertext.

HTTPS gives three things at once:
1. **Confidentiality** — nobody can read what you're sending.
2. **Integrity** — nobody can silently modify it.
3. **Authentication** — you know you're talking to the real server, not an impostor.

## A complete sample program — make and inspect an HTTP request

This Dart script issues an HTTP request and prints every detail — the exact bytes on the wire, the status code, the headers, timing:
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Putting sensitive data in the URL.**

URLs land in server logs, browser history, `Referer` headers, and analytics tools. Never put tokens, passwords, credit-card numbers, or personal IDs in the query string. Use headers or the request body.

**Mistake 2: Ignoring status codes and returning `200 OK` for errors.**

Some APIs return `200 OK` with `{ "error": "not_found" }` in the body. This breaks caching, monitoring, and retry logic — clients treat 200 as success. Use the correct 4xx/5xx code AND include an error body.

**Mistake 3: Retrying POST requests without an idempotency key.**

If a POST /orders times out, retrying may create a second order. Add an `Idempotency-Key: <uuid>` header — the server can deduplicate. This is exactly what Stripe requires.
## Best practices
**1. Design URLs around nouns, not verbs.** `/users/42/orders` is REST-ful. `/getOrdersForUser?id=42` is not — the verb belongs in the HTTP method.
**2. Always version your API.** Ship it as `/v1/...` from day one. Otherwise your first breaking change will hurt every client.

**3. Return the correct status code AND a machine-readable error body.** Something like `{ "code": "user_not_found", "message": "...", "request_id": "..." }`. Log the request_id server-side for tracing.
**4. Set timeouts on every HTTP call.** Never let a hanging request block your app forever.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Print all headers.** Write a script that GETs a URL and prints every response header along with the status code.

**Why:** You'll see the invisible details every server sends — `Cache-Control`, `Set-Cookie`, `ETag`, `Server` — that most beginners never notice.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Conditional GET.** Fetch a URL. Store its `ETag` header. Fetch again with `If-None-Match: <that etag>`. Confirm the server returns `304 Not Modified` (empty body — huge bandwidth saving).

**Why:** Real caching in the wild uses exactly this pattern.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Mini REST API.** Build a small HTTP server for a TODO list with `GET /todos`, `POST /todos`, `PUT /todos/:id`, `DELETE /todos/:id`. Return the right status codes (200, 201, 204, 404). Use in-memory storage — no database needed.

**Why:** You'll internalize the resource model that every REST API on Earth uses.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Token-bucket rate limiter.** Extend your TODO API so each client IP can only make 10 requests per minute; excess requests get `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header.

**Why:** Rate limiting is a real-world skill; getting it wrong breaks production.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Is PUT idempotent? Is POST? Explain.**

**A:** **PUT is idempotent**: `PUT /users/42 { name: "Ada" }` twice leaves the user's name as 'Ada' — same result as once. **POST is not idempotent**: `POST /orders` twice usually creates two orders. This is why retry logic must be method-aware — you can safely retry a PUT after a timeout, but not a POST unless you use an idempotency key. DELETE is also idempotent: deleting a resource twice leaves it deleted.

**Q2: What's the difference between 401 and 403?**

**A:** **401 Unauthorized** means the server couldn't authenticate you — you didn't send credentials, or they were wrong. The fix is to log in (or send a token). **403 Forbidden** means the server knows exactly who you are, but you're not allowed to perform this action. The fix is not more login — the user just doesn't have permission. In practice: 401 means 'try again with credentials'; 403 means 'don't bother, ask an admin.'

**Q3: What is HTTP statelessness and how do we still 'stay logged in'?**

**A:** HTTP itself keeps no memory between requests — every one stands alone. Login state is layered on top using **cookies**, **tokens**, or **sessions**. The server sends a session ID (cookie) or a signed token (JWT) after login. The client sends it back on every request. The server verifies and treats you as authenticated. So HTTP is still stateless — the state lives in what the client presents each time.

**Q4: What problem does HTTP/2 solve that HTTP/1.1 had?**

**A:** HTTP/1.1 had **head-of-line blocking**: even with persistent connections, requests over the same connection were processed in order — a slow request would block later ones. Browsers worked around this by opening 6 parallel TCP connections per host. HTTP/2 introduced **multiplexed streams** on a single TCP connection: many requests interleave in parallel, so one slow response doesn't block others. It also added binary framing (faster to parse) and header compression (huge win — cookies and auth headers repeat on every request).

**Q5: How does HTTPS differ from HTTP at the protocol level?**

**A:** HTTPS is the same HTTP protocol, but wrapped in a **TLS-encrypted tunnel**. Before any HTTP is exchanged, the client and server do a TLS handshake: the server presents an X.509 certificate signed by a trusted CA, the client verifies it, and they agree on a symmetric session key. After the handshake, all HTTP data — headers, body, URLs — is encrypted. This gives confidentiality (nobody can read the traffic), integrity (nobody can modify it), and authentication (you know you're talking to the real server). The trade-off is a small handshake cost, mitigated in TLS 1.3 with fewer round trips and 0-RTT resumption.

**Q6: What makes an API RESTful?**

**A:** REST (Roy Fielding) has six constraints, but for practical interviews the essence is: **resource-oriented URLs (nouns, not verbs), correct HTTP methods, correct status codes, stateless server, and cacheable responses.** So `GET /users/42` is REST; `POST /getUser?id=42` is not. A truly REST-ful API also uses hypermedia (HATEOAS) — the server tells the client what actions are available next — but in the real world 'Level 2' REST (URLs + methods + status codes) is what almost everyone actually ships.

**Q7: How does an ETag / 304 save bandwidth?**

**A:** On a successful GET, the server sends an `ETag` header — a hash or version string identifying the response. The client stores the ETag with the cached response. On the next request the client sends `If-None-Match: <etag>`. If the resource hasn't changed, the server replies **304 Not Modified** with no body — telling the client 'your cached version is still fresh.' No wasted bandwidth on unchanged data. Every browser and every CDN uses this.

## Real-world examples

- **Stripe's API** is the gold standard: idempotency keys on POST, correct status codes, precise error bodies, versioning via a `Stripe-Version` header. Read their docs before any API-design interview.
- **GitHub's API** uses ETags aggressively; polling for changes with `If-None-Match` costs almost nothing.
- **Zomato / Swiggy** menus are cached with `Cache-Control` — that's why they load instantly on your phone even on slow networks.
- **Every 502 you've ever seen on a website** is a reverse proxy (Nginx, CDN) telling you the upstream server failed.

## Advanced notes

- **CORS** — the browser blocks cross-origin requests unless the server allows them with headers like `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. Half of 'why doesn't my API work' questions on the internet are CORS.
- **HTTP/3 + QUIC** — TCP has head-of-line blocking at the transport level; QUIC (UDP-based) fixes it. Great for mobile with packet loss.
- **Server-sent events (SSE) & WebSocket** — for real-time updates without polling.
- **`Cache-Control: no-store` vs `no-cache` vs `private`** — they mean different things; mis-configuring them causes stale data or bloated bandwidth.
- **gRPC** rides on top of HTTP/2 for binary, typed RPCs. Same transport, very different application layer.

flashcards.back: GET, HEAD, OPTIONS — they don't change server state.
GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. POST and PATCH are NOT.
401 = not authenticated (log in). 403 = authenticated but not allowed (ask an admin).
The server saying 'your cached copy is still fresh' — no body sent.
Multiplexed streams, binary framing, header compression — no more head-of-line blocking at the app layer.

quiz.explain: POST creates something new each time; repeated calls have additional effects.

quiz.q: The right response for 'we hit our rate limit' is…
quiz.explain: 429 Too Many Requests exists exactly for this.

challenges.text: Write a script that GETs 3 different URLs and prints their status codes + Content-Type + size in bytes.
Implement Conditional GET using `ETag` / `If-None-Match`; count how many 304s you get.
Build a POST client that submits form-encoded data + JSON data, and shows the server sees both.
Tiny REST API for a TODO list — GET/POST/PUT/DELETE with correct status codes.
Add HTTP Basic auth (base64 of `user:pass`) to your TODO API.
Add token-bucket rate limiting to the TODO API: 10 requests/min per client IP, returning 429 with `Retry-After`.
